Guide May 28, 2026 • RecordWatchdog Team

A Beginner's Guide to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) Requests

The FOIA is a powerful tool for accessing federal government records. Learn how to draft, submit, and track an effective request.

Advertisement

Google AdSense Unit

What is the Freedom of Information Act?

The Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) is a foundational federal law that gives you the legal right to request access to records from any federal agency. It is a vital tool for journalists, academic researchers, and engaged citizens seeking government transparency.

What Can You Legally Request?

You can request emails, internal memos, statistical reports, and data held by federal agencies (e.g., FBI, EPA, FDA). However, the law includes nine specific exemptions. You cannot request classified national defense information, confidential commercial data, personal privacy records, or active law enforcement investigatory files.

How to Submit a Successful Request

  • Identify the Agency: Determine which specific federal agency is most likely to hold the records you seek.
  • Be Highly Specific: Vague requests are often rejected or delayed. Include specific dates, names of individuals, document types, and subject matter.
  • Submit Officially: Submit your request through the agency's dedicated online FOIA portal or via certified mail.
  • Prepare for Fees: Be prepared to pay reasonable search and duplication fees, though fee waivers are often available for journalists and academic researchers.

State-Level Equivalents

Remember, the federal FOIA only applies to federal agencies. Each individual state has its own "Sunshine Law" or public records act for accessing state and local government documents. The processes and response times vary significantly by state.

Disclaimer

The information provided in this article is for general informational and educational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Public record systems and laws vary by jurisdiction and are subject to change. Always verify information directly with the official government agency or consult with a qualified attorney. RecordWatchdog is not a consumer reporting agency.
